concepts.title: Motion in One Dimension
concepts.summary: Describing position, velocity, and acceleration along a straight line using graphs and equations.
concepts.history_text: Galileo pioneered the study of uniformly accelerated motion through inclined plane experiments.

sections.content:
Position \(x\) is a location relative to a chosen origin. **Displacement** \(\Delta x = x_f - x_i\) is a vector quantity. Distance is the scalar total length traveled.

Example: Walking 4 m east then 2 m west gives \(\Delta x = +2\) m but distance = 6 m.
If you complete one full lap on a 400 m track, what is your displacement? How does this illustrate the difference between vector and scalar quantities?
Use a meter stick or motion sensor to walk in a straight line. Record positions at regular time intervals and plot a position-time graph.

**Average velocity** \(v_{avg} = \frac{\Delta x}{\Delta t}\). **Instantaneous velocity** is the slope of the position-time graph at a point.

Velocity is a vector; speed is its magnitude.
Can an object have constant speed but changing velocity? Provide an everyday example.

Acceleration \(a = \frac{\Delta v}{\Delta t}\). On velocity-time graphs, slope gives acceleration and area gives displacement.

Constant acceleration produces parabolic position-time graphs.
Use a dynamics cart on a slightly inclined track with a motion sensor. Analyze position, velocity, and acceleration graphs.

The four key equations:
1. \(v = v_0 + a t\)
2. \(\Delta x = v_0 t + \frac{1}{2} a t^2\)
3. \(v^2 = v_0^2 + 2 a \Delta x\)
4. \(\Delta x = \frac{v + v_0}{2} t\)

These assume constant acceleration.
Near Earth's surface, free-falling objects accelerate at \(g \approx 9.8\) m/s² downward (ignoring air resistance). Use kinematic equations with \(a = -g\).
Drop objects of different masses from the same height (use video analysis or stopwatch). Compare fall times.
Why do a feather and a hammer fall at different rates on Earth but would fall together on the Moon?

concepts.title: Motion in Two Dimensions
concepts.summary: Vectors, projectile motion, and relative motion.
concepts.history_text: Newton extended Galileo's ideas using vector components.

sections.content: Resolve vectors: \(v_x = v \cos \theta\), \(v_y = v \sin \theta\). Add by components: \(R_x = A_x + B_x\).
Use graph paper or a vector applet to add displacement vectors for a treasure hunt scenario.
Horizontal velocity is constant. Vertical motion: \(y = \frac{1}{2} (-g) t^2\). Time of flight depends only on height.
Range \(R = \frac{v_0^2 \sin 2\theta}{g}\), maximum at 45°. Vertical and horizontal motions are independent.
At what launch angle is the range maximized? What trade-off occurs for maximum height?
Launch a projectile (e.g., ball from ramp or launcher) at different angles. Measure range and compare to calculations.
Velocity of A relative to B: \(\vec{v}_{A/B} = \vec{v}_{A} - \vec{v}_{B}\). Classic example: boat crossing a river.

concepts.title: Newton's Laws of Motion
concepts.summary: Forces and their effects on motion.
concepts.history_text: Isaac Newton formalized these in *Principia Mathematica* (1687).

sections.content: An object remains at rest or in uniform motion unless acted on by a net external force. Inertia is resistance to change in motion.
**\(\vec{F}_{net} = m \vec{a}\)**. Free-body diagrams isolate forces. Units: 1 N = 1 kg·m/s².
Vary hanging mass on a pulley system attached to a cart. Measure acceleration and plot F vs. a.
Forces come in pairs: \(\vec{F}_{AB} = -\vec{F}_{BA}\). Example: rocket propulsion or walking.
When you jump, you push Earth down—why don't we notice Earth moving?
For a system, treat external forces only. Center of mass accelerates as if all mass were there: \(M \vec{a}_{cm} = \vec{F}_{ext}\).

concepts.title: Applications of Newton's Laws
concepts.summary: Friction, tension, normal force, and inclined planes.
concepts.history_text: These applications were refined through experiments by many scientists post-Newton.

sections.content: Draw all forces. For equilibrium: \(\sum F_x = 0\), \(\sum F_y = 0\).
Resolve gravity: parallel component \(mg \sin \theta\), perpendicular \(mg \cos \theta\).
Release a cart on an inclined plane. Measure acceleration and verify \(a = g \sin \theta\) (low friction).
\(f_s \leq \mu_s N\), \(f_k = \mu_k N\). Static friction prevents motion; kinetic opposes it.
Pull a block with a spring scale on different surfaces. Find coefficients of friction.
Ideal strings transmit tension uniformly. Atwood's machine: acceleration depends on mass difference.

concepts.title: Circular Motion
concepts.summary: Uniform circular motion and centripetal force.
concepts.history_text: Newton connected circular motion to his law of gravitation.

sections.content: Centripetal acceleration \(a_c = \frac{v^2}{r}\). Centripetal force \(F_c = \frac{m v^2}{r}\) toward center.
Banking angle reduces reliance on friction: \(\tan \theta = \frac{v^2}{r g}\).
At the top of a vertical loop, why can a car stay on track even if speed seems low?

concepts.title: Work and the Work-Energy Theorem
concepts.summary: Energy transfer via force over distance.
concepts.history_text: The work-energy theorem unifies earlier ideas about motion and forces.

lessons.title: Work by Constant and Variable Forces

sections.content: Work \(W = \vec{F} \cdot \vec{d} = F d \cos \theta\). Area under force-displacement graph.
Net work equals change in kinetic energy: \(W_{net} = \Delta KE = \frac{1}{2} m v_f^2 - \frac{1}{2} m v_i^2\).
Measure work done by a spring launcher on a cart and compare to gained kinetic energy.
Gravitational PE: \(U_g = m g h\). Spring PE: \(U_s = \frac{1}{2} k x^2\).
When only conservative forces act: \(KE_i + PE_i = KE_f + PE_f\).
A roller coaster loses energy to friction. How does this affect maximum height on subsequent hills?
Pendulum or ramp experiment: compare initial PE to final KE (account for losses).
Work by friction or drag dissipates mechanical energy as thermal energy.

concepts.title: Power
concepts.history_text: James Watt defined horsepower while improving steam engines.

sections.content: Power \(P = \frac{W}{t} = F v\) (instantaneous). Efficiency = useful output / total input.

concepts.title: Momentum, Impulse, and Collisions
concepts.summary: Mass in motion and changes due to forces.
concepts.history_text: Conservation follows directly from Newton's third law.

sections.content: Momentum \(\vec{p} = m \vec{v}\). Impulse \(\vec{J} = \vec{F} \Delta t = \Delta \vec{p}\).
Isolated system: \(\vec{p}_{total,i} = \vec{p}_{total,f}\).
Elastic and inelastic collisions using air track or low-friction carts. Verify momentum conservation.
Elastic: both momentum and KE conserved. Inelastic: momentum conserved, KE not.
In a perfectly inelastic collision, why is kinetic energy always lost?
The center of mass moves as if all external forces act on it.

concepts.title: Rotational Kinematics
concepts.history_text: Euler and others developed rotational descriptions.

sections.content: Relations: \(s = r \theta\), \(v = r \omega\), \(a_t = r \alpha\). Equations mirror linear ones with angular variables.

concepts.title: Torque and Rotational Dynamics
concepts.history_text: The torque concept often leads to the 'Torque Paradox' for students.

sections.content: Torque \(\tau = r F \sin \theta\) or \(\vec{\tau} = \vec{r} \times \vec{F}\). \(\tau_{net} = I \alpha\).
Why does a small force far from the pivot produce more torque than a large force near it? Think of opening a door.
I = Σ m r². Depends on mass distribution. Parallel-axis theorem: \(I = I_{cm} + M d^2\).
Compare rolling times of different objects (hoop vs. solid cylinder) down a ramp.
For equilibrium: net torque = 0 and net force = 0 (seesaw, ladder problems).

concepts.title: Angular Momentum
concepts.history_text: Described in planetary motion and rigid body dynamics.

sections.content: L = I ω or \(\vec{L} = \vec{r} \times \vec{p}\). Conserved when net external torque is zero.
Rotating stool with dumbbells: pull arms in and observe speed increase.
Explain why figure skaters spin faster when pulling in their arms.
Combines translation and rotation: \(v = r \omega\), total KE = \(\frac{1}{2} m v^2 + \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2\).
Restoring force F = -kx leads to \(a = -\frac{k}{m} x\). Period \(T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{m}{k}}\).
Hang different masses on a spring. Measure periods and verify relationship with mass.
For small angles: \(T \approx 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{L}{g}}\). Independent of mass.
Total energy conserved: max KE at equilibrium, max PE at amplitude.

concepts.title: Universal Gravitation
concepts.history_text: Newton linked falling objects to planetary orbits.

sections.content: F = G \frac{m_1 m_2}{r^2}. Gravitational field strength g = G M / r².
For circular orbits: \(\frac{G M m}{r^2} = \frac{m v^2}{r}\). Kepler's 3rd law: T² ∝ r³.
Astronauts in orbit feel weightless—why is gravity still acting strongly?
U = -G \frac{m_1 m_2}{r} (negative because work is done by gravity as objects approach).
